--- title: "Applying transformers to columns" format: html: toc: true revealjs: slide-number: true toc: false code-fold: false code-tools: true --- ## Why Column-Specific Transformers? Different columns need different transformations: - `StandardScaler` works only on numeric columns - `OneHotEncoder` works only on categorical columns - Some columns need no transformation ## Selection Operations `SelectCols` and `DropCols` filter columns based on rules: ::: {#99a4cc11 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} from skrub import SelectCols, DropCols import pandas as pd df = pd.DataFrame({ "date": ["03 January 2023", "04 February 2023"], "city": ["Paris", "London"], "values": [10, 20] }) ``` ::: ## Selection Operations {auto-animate="true"} ::: {#676d5b77 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} # By name SelectCols("date").fit_transform(df) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2} ```{=html}
date
0 03 January 2023
1 04 February 2023
``` ::: ::: ## Selection Operations {auto-animate="true"} ::: {#05c32bdc .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} DropCols("date").fit_transform(df) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ```{=html}
city values
0 Paris 10
1 London 20
``` ::: ::: More advanced selection rules in the next chapter! ## Applying Transformers to Columns `ApplyToCols` applies a transformer to columns that can be selected by name, or according to specific rules. ::: {#4c22bab5 .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} from skrub import ApplyToCols import skrub.selectors as s from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder one_hot = ApplyToCols(OneHotEncoder(sparse_output=False), cols="city") transformed = one_hot.fit_transform(df) transformed ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ```{=html}
date values city_London city_Paris
0 03 January 2023 10 0.0 1.0
1 04 February 2023 20 1.0 0.0
``` ::: ::: ## Example: `ApplyToCols` with a single column transformer ![](./images/ex_ApplyToCols.png) ## Example: `ApplyToCols` with a regular transformer ![](./images/ex_ApplyToFrame.png) ## Exclude specific columns For example, scale all numeric columns except for an integer ID. ::: {#5d77324f .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_id = pd.DataFrame(dict(id=[1000, 2000], A=[-10.0, 10.0], B=[-10.0, 0.0], C=[19, 20])) df_id ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=5} ```{=html}
id A B C
0 1000 -10.0 -10.0 19
1 2000 10.0 0.0 20
``` ::: ::: ::: {#7270193f .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler exc_scaler = ApplyToCols(StandardScaler(), exclude_cols="id") exc_scaler.fit_transform(df_id) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=6} ```{=html}
id A B C
0 1000 -1.0 -1.0 -1.0
1 2000 1.0 1.0 1.0
``` ::: ::: ## The `allow_reject` Parameter When `allow_reject=True`, columns that can't be transformed are passed through: ::: {#f785c2e2 .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} from skrub import ApplyToCols, ToDatetime with_reject = ApplyToCols(ToDatetime(), allow_reject=True) result = with_reject.fit_transform(df) ``` ::: This is useful when you don't know if a column may be rejected or not (common when parsing types). ## The `allow_reject` Parameter When `allow_reject=False`, columns that can't be transformed raise an exception: ::: {#828a1b7a .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} from skrub import ApplyToCols, ToDatetime with_reject = ApplyToCols(ToDatetime(), allow_reject=False) result = with_reject.fit_transform(df) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ::: {.ansi-escaped-output} ```{=html}

``` ::: ::: ::: ## Build your own `SingleColumnTransformer` {.smaller} Consider an example like this: ::: {#9ab3a406 .cell execution_count=9} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd df = pd.DataFrame({'sent': ["AB123", "BD601", "HS014"], 'received': ["AB145", "DU393", "WB988"]}) df ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=9} ```{=html}
sent received
0 AB123 AB145
1 BD601 DU393
2 HS014 WB988
``` ::: ::: Given a code like `AB123`, we want to separate the first two letters from the digits. ## Build your own `SingleColumnTransformer` {.smaller auto-animate="true"} The `SingleColumnTransformer` allows to define a transformer with custom rules, while `RejectColumn` gives control over the behavior in presence of invalid inputs. ::: {#a8e2084f .cell execution_count=10} ``` {.python .cell-code} from skrub.core import RejectColumn, SingleColumnTransformer class ZipcodeParser(SingleColumnTransformer): def fit_transform(self, X, y=None): col_name = X.name if any(X.map(len) != 5): raise RejectColumn("This transformer only takes zip codes of length 5.") else: # extract the first two characters letters = X.map(lambda s: s[:2]) try: # extract the last 3 characters and try to convert to int numbers = X.map(lambda s: int(s[2:])) except: raise RejectColumn( "Input zip codes must consist of two letters followed by three numbers." ) return pd.DataFrame( {f"{col_name}_letters": letters, f"{col_name}_numbers": numbers} ) ``` ::: ## Build your own `SingleColumnTransformer` {auto-animate="true"} ::: {#4960f6a1 .cell execution_count=11} ``` {.python .cell-code} parser = ApplyToCols(ZipcodeParser(), allow_reject=True) parser.fit_transform(df) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=11} ```{=html}
sent_letters sent_numbers received_letters received_numbers
0 AB 123 AB 145
1 BD 601 DU 393
2 HS 14 WB 988
``` ::: ::: ## Chaining Transformers Use scikit-learn pipelines to concatenate multiple transformers, even when wrapped in `ApplyToCols`: ```{.python} from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder select = SelectCols("department-name") encode = ApplyToCols(OneHotEncoder(sparse_output=False)) reduce = ApplyToCols(PCA(n_components=2)) transform = make_pipeline(select, encode, reduce) ``` ::: {.callout-tip} Remember that columns that are not selected pass through without any change. ::: ## Order Matters! {.smaller} Transformers should be ordered carefully: ```{.python} # Encode first, then scale transform_1 = make_pipeline( ApplyToCols(OneHotEncoder(sparse_output=False), cols=s.string()), ApplyToCols(StandardScaler(), cols=s.numeric()) ) # vs. Scale first, then encode transform_2 = make_pipeline( ApplyToCols(StandardScaler(), cols=s.numeric()), ApplyToCols(OneHotEncoder(sparse_output=False), cols=s.string()) ) ``` ## What we have seen in this chapter - `SelectCols` / `DropCols`: Filter columns with the `cols` parameter - `ApplyToCols`: Apply a transformer to columns selected with `cols`, exclude with `exclude_cols` - `SingleColumnTransformer`: Create a transformer with custom rules - `allow_reject`: Reject columns that cannot be treated - Chain transformers with scikit-learn pipelines
